facts_disclosed: Lives in a 1-bedroom in JLT (Jumeirah Lake Towers) — 35-minute commute

turns.content: Ugh. Can I vent for a second? I'm sitting in traffic right now and I want to scream.
Absolutely, go ahead. Traffic frustration is completely valid. What's happening out there?

So I live in JLT — Jumeirah Lake Towers — and my clinic is on the other side of the city. On a GOOD day the commute is like 35 minutes but today? Gente, I've been in this car for almost an hour and I'm not even at the interchange yet. There was some accident on Sheikh Zayed Road and everything is just stopped. I'm going to be late for my first patient!!
That sounds incredibly frustrating, especially when you have patients waiting. Is there an alternative route you could take to bypass the accident, or is it too late for that at this point?

I tried to go through Al Khail Road but EVERYONE had the same idea so now that's jammed too haha. It's like this every time something happens — one accident and the whole city collapses. I love Dubai but ai meu Deus the traffic infrastructure was NOT built for this many people. Back in Sao Paulo the traffic was terrible too but at least we had the metro going everywhere!

Dubai's road dependency is definitely one of its weak points — when one main artery gets blocked, the ripple effect is massive because there aren't enough alternatives. Have you looked into whether the metro could work for any part of your commute? JLT has a metro station, right?

It does! DMCC station is close to my building. But the problem is my clinic isn't near any metro station — I'd still need to take a taxi from the nearest stop and then it ends up being the same time or longer. Plus I carry a lot of stuff to work, resistance bands, my own foam roller, sometimes I bring my Theragun for special cases. I look like I'm moving house every morning haha.

Ha, the image of you hauling a bag of therapy equipment onto the metro is pretty funny. But I get it — if the last-mile connection doesn't work, the metro saves you nothing. Have you considered keeping some of your equipment at the clinic permanently so you wouldn't need to carry it? That might at least open up the metro option for days like today.

I actually do keep most things at the clinic but I have my personal stuff that I like using because it's MINE you know? The clinic equipment is fine but my Theragun is the Pro model and theirs is the basic one, and my resistance bands are the good ones from Brazil. I'm particular about my tools haha, it's a PT thing.

That makes total sense — you want the tools you trust, especially when you're working on athletes. Have you thought about the bigger picture though? If 35 minutes is your baseline and it regularly spikes to an hour, that's a lot of your life spent in traffic. Is moving closer to the clinic something you've ever considered?

I think about it ALL the time querida. But my 1-bedroom in JLT is actually a really good deal — the rent is reasonable for Dubai and the apartment itself is nice. I have a balcony with a lake view, the building gym is decent, and there's like fifteen restaurants downstairs. If I move closer to the clinic the apartments are either tiny or expensive. Dubai real estate is no joke!!

That's a real tradeoff — you've got a good living situation with amenities you actually use, and giving that up for a shorter commute might mean downgrading in other ways. A few ideas that might help with the commute pain:

1. **Shift your hours** — Could you start earlier or later to miss peak traffic? Even 30 minutes can make a huge difference on Sheikh Zayed Road.
2. **Audio investment** — Podcasts, audiobooks, or language apps can make the drive feel productive instead of wasted.
3. **Flexible days** — If your clinic allows it, could you do admin work or telehealth consultations from home one day a week?

Would any of those work with your schedule?

The shifting hours idea is actually smart! My first patient is usually at 8:30 and I leave at 7:45. If I left at 7:15 the roads might be better and I could use the extra time to prep. And I already listen to podcasts — I'm obsessed with this biomechanics podcast, I learn so much. But telehealth doesn't really work for PT haha, I need to put my hands on people! You can't assess a rotator cuff through a screen meu bem.

Fair point — physical therapy is about as hands-on as it gets! Leaving 30 minutes earlier could genuinely transform your commute though. Pre-peak traffic on Sheikh Zayed is a completely different road than peak traffic. You might even get there in 25 minutes and have time for your own warm-up or a coffee before the day starts.

And on days like today when accidents happen regardless — at least you'd have a buffer built in. Nothing worse than stress-driving and then having to immediately switch into calm therapist mode for your first patient.

That is SO true. I literally arrive stressed and then I have to be all zen and healing for my patients haha. Okay I'm going to try leaving at 7:15 this week and see if it helps. Oh — traffic is finally moving a little! I think I see the clinic exit. Only 20 minutes late, nossa. Thank you for letting me vent, I feel better already!

Glad the traffic is breaking up! Twenty minutes late is recoverable. Try the earlier departure this week and see how it goes — even a few stress-free commutes might change your whole morning energy. Good luck with your patients today, and drive safe!
